articles.headline: 90 Minutes of Lifting Per Week Just Became Your Longevity Number
articles.body: Forget the gym rat mentality. A sweeping 30-year study of 147,000 people just revealed something elegant: 90 to 120 minutes of strength training weekly cuts your risk of early death, with benefits extending to heart and brain health. More than that? No additional edge. This wasn't just lifting—the study tracked combined resistance training across various forms. When combined with aerobic exercise, the benefits stacked even higher. The message: consistent, moderate strength work beats extremes every time.
articles.takeaway: Aim for 90-120 minutes of strength work per week. Consistency beats volume. Combine it with cardio for maximum longevity gains.

articles.headline: One Nutrient May Hold the Key to Slowing Your Cells' Aging Clock
articles.body: Scientists just identified a major culprit in how your cells lose energy as you age—and it's fixable. Declining phosphatidylcholine levels trigger mitochondrial decline, essentially starving your cells of power. The breakthrough: boosting this nutrient through diet restored mitochondrial function to more youthful levels in both organisms and human cell cultures. This wasn't a lab curiosity. Researchers tested it across worms, human data, and cell models. The implication? Some aging processes you thought were fixed may be reversible with the right nutrient strategy.
articles.takeaway: Phosphatidylcholine supports mitochondrial health and energy production. While supplementation research is emerging, dietary sources matter for cellular aging.

articles.topic: Cold water immersion muscle soreness timing
articles.headline: Cold Water Recovery Works, But There's a 24-Hour Window You Need to Know About
articles.body: Cold water immersion actually works for recovery—but not how most people use it. A new meta-analysis of 30 controlled trials found it meaningfully reduced muscle soreness and muscle damage markers like creatine kinase. The catch: benefits were concentrated in the first 24 hours post-exercise and faded by 48-72 hours. Single sessions aren't a magic fix. If you're icing, do it immediately after your workout. Expecting one cold plunge to carry recovery forward for days? That's not how the physiology works.
articles.takeaway: Use cold water within the first hour post-workout for soreness reduction. Don't rely on it beyond 24 hours. Repeat sessions work better than one-offs.
articles.source: Frontiers in Sports and Active Living
articles.pexelsQuery: cold water ice bath athlete recovery

articles.headline: Your Brain Actually Rewires When You Handle Stress Well
articles.body: Stress resilience isn't about staying calm—it's about how your brain wires itself in response to challenge. New research from Florida International University reveals that how you perceive stress directly shapes your neural wiring and stress response. Participants who engaged with stressful stimuli showed measurable brain-level adaptations. The takeaway isn't that stress is good. It's that your subjective experience of stress—whether you see it as a threat or a challenge—literally changes your neurobiology. Resilience is built through engagement, not avoidance.
articles.takeaway: Reframe stress as adaptive. Directly engaging challenges builds neural resilience better than avoidance strategies.
articles.pexelsQuery: person meditating stress relief mental health

quote.text: The more you can engage in activities that make you feel good, the more resilient you become to stress.
quote.author: Dr. Andrew Huberman
quote.context: Stanford neuroscientist and neurobiology researcher studying stress, resilience, and optimal performance

workout.title: Foundation Four: Bodyweight Movement Mastery
workout.tip: Prioritize slow, controlled movement over speed. If you can't do a push-up from your toes, use your knees—there's no shame in regression. Nail the pattern first, then add intensity.
workout.warmup: 3 min: 5 arm circles each direction, 10 cat-cow stretches, 5 inchworms, 10 bodyweight squats, 5 push-ups

exercises.name: Goblet-Hold Air Squat
exercises.notes: Clasp hands at chest as if holding a weight. Descend until thighs pass parallel, chest upright. This teaches squat pattern without load.

exercises.name: Glute Bridge
exercises.notes: Lie flat, feet flat under knees, drive hips up until body is straight line. Squeeze glutes at top for 1 second. Teaches hip extension hinge pattern.

exercises.name: Incline Push-Up
exercises.notes: Hands on a bench, chair, or countertop. Lower chest to surface, elbows at 45°. Scale the movement by raising your hands higher if needed.

exercises.name: Dead Hang or Scapular Pull
exercises.notes: If you have a pull-up bar: hang for time, relax shoulders. If no bar: use resistance band around a door frame and practice pulling shoulder blades down and back.

workout.cooldown: 4 min: quad stretch 30 sec each leg, forward fold hamstring 30 sec, child's pose 30 sec, cat-cow 5 reps, standing chest opener 30 sec each side

meal.title: Seared Beef Tenderloin with Roasted Potatoes & Green Beans
meal.instructions:
1. Cube potatoes into 1-inch pieces and toss with 0.5 tbsp olive oil, salt, and pepper.
2. Roast at 400°F for 12 minutes until golden; add green beans tossed in remaining oil and cook 6 more minutes.
3. Season beef tenderloin with salt, pepper, and garlic powder.
4. Sear in a hot skillet over medium-high heat for 3–4 minutes per side (medium-rare).
5. Rest 2 minutes, slice, and plate alongside potatoes and green beans. Eat 3–4 hours before race start.
meal.tip: A more substantial race-day meal for triathletes who prefer a heavier breakfast or brunch 3–4 hours before competition. Lean beef provides heme iron and sustained amino acids without digestive lag.
